quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Entropy"?

options.text: A measure of disorder or the number of microstates: S = k ln Ω; always increases in irreversible processes
Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier
When a driving frequency matches an object's natural frequency, the amplitude of oscillation increases dramatically
v_rms = √(3kT/m) = √(3RT/M); average molecular speed increases with temperature and decreases with molecular mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Entropy" is defined as: A measure of disorder or the number of microstates: S = k ln Ω; always increases in irreversible processes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Second Law of Thermodynamics"?

options.text: Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows
Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly
1/f = (n-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂); relates focal length to the lens material (n) and surface curvatures (R₁, R₂)
The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases; natural processes are irreversible

quiz_questions.explanation: "Second Law of Thermodynamics" is defined as: The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases; natural processes are irreversible. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Heat engine efficiency"?

options.text: V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C)
ΣI_in = ΣI_out at any junction; based on conservation of charge
η = W/Q_H = 1 - Q_C/Q_H; no engine can convert all heat to work (η < 1 always)
Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars

quiz_questions.explanation: "Heat engine efficiency" is defined as: η = W/Q_H = 1 - Q_C/Q_H; no engine can convert all heat to work (η < 1 always). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Carnot efficiency"?

options.text: v_rms = √(3kT/m) = √(3RT/M); average molecular speed increases with temperature and decreases with molecular mass
Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy)
η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H; the maximum possible efficiency for an engine operating between temperatures T_H and T_C (in Kelvin)
f_observed = f_source · (v ± v_observer)/(v ∓ v_source); approaching gives higher pitch, receding gives lower pitch

quiz_questions.explanation: "Carnot efficiency" is defined as: η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H; the maximum possible efficiency for an engine operating between temperatures T_H and T_C (in Kelvin). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Reversible vs. irreversible"?

options.text: Angular momentum L = nℏ (n = 1,2,3,...); equivalently, nλ = 2πr (standing wave condition for electron orbit)
Diagnostic imaging (gamma cameras, PET) and therapy (targeted radiation, brachytherapy) using radioactive isotopes
Intensity I ∝ A²; for spherical waves: I = P/(4πr²), intensity decreases as 1/r²
Reversible: idealized, infinitely slow, maximum work; irreversible: real, finite speed, always produces less work and more entropy

quiz_questions.explanation: "Reversible vs. irreversible" is defined as: Reversible: idealized, infinitely slow, maximum work; irreversible: real, finite speed, always produces less work and more entropy. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A measure of disorder or the number of microstates: S = k ln Ω; always increases in irreversible processes"?

options.text: Photon emission
Resonance
Dielectric constant (κ)
Entropy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Entropy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases; natural processes are irreversible"?

options.text: Second Law of Thermodynamics
Thomson's experiment
Rayleigh criterion
Rutherford model

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Second Law of Thermodynamics".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "η = W/Q_H = 1 - Q_C/Q_H; no engine can convert all heat to work (η < 1 always)"?

options.text: Heisenberg uncertainty principle
Law of reflection
Chromatic aberration
Heat engine efficiency

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Heat engine efficiency".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H; the maximum possible efficiency for an engine operating between temperatures T_H and T_C (in Kelvin)"?

options.text: Carnot efficiency
Electric field
Magnetic force on a charge
Lens power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Carnot efficiency".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Reversible: idealized, infinitely slow, maximum work; irreversible: real, finite speed, always produces less work and more entropy"?

options.text: Compound microscope
Ohm's law
Right-hand rule (force)
Reversible vs. irreversible

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Reversible vs. irreversible".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics is accurate?

options.text: The law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection (θᵢ = θᵣ), measured from the normal.
Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics governs the direction of natural processes.
The Rydberg formula for hydrogen: 1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²), where R_H = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹.
Fluids and Pressure examines the behavior of liquids and gases under pressure.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics?

options.text: The standing-wave condition (electron wavelength must fit the orbital circumference: nλ = 2πr) explains quantization.
Electromagnetic interactions are one of the four fundamental forces of nature.
Electric Circuits involve the flow of charge through conductive paths driven by a potential difference.
Entropy (S) quantifies disorder: ΔS ≥ 0 for any isolated system.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics?

options.text: Electrons and Atomic Models traces the evolution of atomic theory from Thomson's plum pudding model to Bohr's quantized orbits to the quantum mechanical model.
The second law states that natural processes increase the total entropy of the universe; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold, never the reverse, without external work.
Mass and energy are interconvertible: the mass defect in nuclear reactions is converted to kinetic energy of products and radiation.
Conservation laws apply: total charge (Z), total mass number (A), total energy, and total momentum are conserved.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics?

options.text: Plane mirrors produce virtual, upright, same-size images located behind the mirror.
EMF can be induced by changing B, changing A, or changing the angle θ between B and the area vector.
Heat engines convert thermal energy to work with efficiency η = W/Q_H = 1 - Q_C/Q_H.
An absorption spectrum shows dark lines at the same wavelengths when continuous light passes through a cool gas—the gas absorbs photons matching its transition energies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics?

options.text: The Carnot efficiency η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H sets the theoretical maximum efficiency.
Wave Properties and Behavior explores the fundamental characteristics common to all waves.
The kinetic theory of gases derives macroscopic properties from molecular motion.
Power dissipated: P = IV = I²R = V²/R.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics?

options.text: Hall effect sensors are used as position detectors in smartphones, automotive systems (crankshaft position), and brushless DC motor commutation
Atomic clocks use the precise frequency of electron transitions in cesium atoms (9.19 GHz) to define the second—accurate to 1 part in 10¹⁴
Flash memory (USB drives, SSDs) stores data using quantum tunneling: electrons tunnel through a thin oxide layer to charge a floating gate, representing binary data
Power plants: coal and nuclear plants operate as heat engines; even the best achieve ~40% efficiency due to thermodynamic limits (Carnot bound)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics?

options.text: Weather balloons expand as they rise because atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, and Boyle's law predicts volume increase at lower pressure
Electric generators convert mechanical energy to electrical energy: rotating coils in magnetic fields produce alternating EMF (AC power) via Faraday's law
Refrigerators work as reverse heat engines, moving heat from cold interior to warm exterior—they require work input, consistent with the second law
Energy storage: supercapacitors bridge the gap between batteries and conventional capacitors, providing rapid charge/discharge for regenerative braking in electric vehicles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Household wiring: outlets are wired in parallel (each at 120V) with circuit breakers in series (trip at 15-20A) to prevent overheating and fires
Arrow of time: entropy increase explains why ice melts in warm rooms but warm rooms don't spontaneously freeze—the second law governs the direction of all natural processes
Nuclear medicine: technetium-99m (a metastable nuclear isomer) is the most widely used medical radioisotope, emitting gamma rays for diagnostic imaging of organs
Induction motors: AC current creates rotating magnetic fields that induce currents in the rotor, producing torque—powering most industrial machinery and household appliances

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics?

options.text: Xerox copiers use electrostatic attraction: a charged drum attracts toner particles to form an image, then heat fuses the toner to paper
Battery internal resistance: real batteries have internal resistance r, so terminal voltage = EMF - Ir; explains why batteries 'die' under heavy load
CRT televisions and oscilloscopes: electron beams are steered by electric and magnetic fields to paint images on a phosphor screen
Power plants: coal and nuclear plants operate as heat engines; even the best achieve ~40% efficiency due to thermodynamic limits (Carnot bound)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics in practice?

options.text: CD/DVD reading: a laser reflects off pits and lands with different path lengths, creating constructive and destructive interference that encodes digital data
Quantum computing uses superposition and entanglement to process information exponentially faster for certain problems (factoring, simulation, optimization)
Refrigerators work as reverse heat engines, moving heat from cold interior to warm exterior—they require work input, consistent with the second law
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics.
